attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with three days of worsening abdominal pain. The pain is constant, moderate in severity, and worst in the epigastrium and right upper quadrant. She reports early satiety and has noticed her abdomen becoming more swollen. She has a medical history of essential thrombocythaemia and takes hydroxyurea. She does not smoke or drink alcohol.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable. Her pulse rate is 95/min, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Her abdomen is tender in the epigastrium and right upper quadrant without guarding. The spleen tip is palpable 3 cm below the costal margin. There is no peripheral oedema.

Laboratory investigations reveal: Haemoglobin 135 g/L (115-165), White cell count 11.2 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0), Platelets 650 x 10^9/L (150-400), Bilirubin 25 μmol/L (0-20), Alkaline phosphatase 145 U/L (30-110), Alanine transferase 65 U/L (0-35), Albumin 38 g/L (35-50).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features suggestive of portal vein thrombosis (PVT), including abdominal pain, splenomegaly, and a significant risk factor (essential thrombocythaemia). The presence of a myeloproliferative disorder significantly increases the risk of splanchnic vein thrombosis, particularly in the setting of thrombocytosis.

Whilst less sensitive and specific compared with CT, abdominal ultrasound with Doppler can detect portal vein thrombosis but may be limited by body habitus, bowel gas, and operator dependency. It is less reliable than CT for determining the extent of thrombosis and identifying other pathology. With ultrasound, the thrombus will appear as a hypo or iso-echoic material inside the vessel and will be more hyperechoic if chronic. This form of imaging is probably the most convenient initial imaging to perform in terms of its availability and immediacy.

CT abdomen with contrast will be the most helpful investigation to provide detailed information. Cross-sectional imaging (angio-CT or angio-MRI) is recommended to confirm the presence of PVT and for complete staging of the thrombus, in terms of its extent and ability to identify any underlying causes or complications. CT also provides excellent visualisation of other abdominal structures that may be contributing to the presentation. However, in the given situation of a patient in the Emergency Department with acute abdominal pain, the most practical initial investigation will be abdominal ultrasonography.

MRI abdomen can provide detailed imaging of portal vein thrombosis but is not typically used as the initial investigation due to longer scanning times, higher cost, and reduced availability compared to CT. It may be useful for follow-up imaging or in cases where CT is contraindicated.

Endoscopic ultrasound would not be an appropriate first-line investigation for suspected portal vein thrombosis. While it can visualise the portal vein, it is invasive and unnecessary when CT can provide the required information.

Upper endoscopy may eventually be needed to assess for varices but is not the appropriate initial investigation when trying to establish the diagnosis of portal vein thrombosis.

After confirming the diagnosis, management would typically include anticoagulation, assessment for underlying prothrombotic conditions if not already known, and monitoring for complications such as varices.
learningPoints: Portal vein thrombosis commonly presents with abdominal pain and features of portal hypertension including splenomegaly and varices.
Cirrhosis, malignancy, and prothrombotic conditions are major risk factors for portal vein thrombosis.
CT with contrast is the preferred initial imaging modality for suspected portal vein thrombosis.
references: Intagliata NM, Caldwell SH, Tripodi A. Diagnosis, Development, and Treatment of Portal Vein Thrombosis in Patients With and Without Cirrhosis. Gastroenterology 2019; 156:1582–99.
Senzolo M, Garcia-Tsao G, Garcia-Pagan JC. Current knowledge and management of portal vein thrombosis in cirrhosis. J Hepatol. 2021; 75:442-53.
